coredata.dc:title: Nb2O5-graphene heterojunction composite with ultrahigh photocatalytic activity for solar light driven photodegradation of ciprofloxacin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Ruiyi, Li
Xiaoyue, Liu
Zaijun, Li
coredata.dc:description: Poor visible light absorption capacity and intrinsic conductivity restrict many applications of Nb2O5 in photocatalysis. The paper describes one solution for synthesizing Nb2O5-graphene photocatalyst with functional graphene quantum dot (GQD). Firstly, Nb(V) was coordinated with GQD to form water-soluble complex. Then, it was bound to graphene sheet via π-π stacking, reduced with ascorbic acid to produce graphene oxide gel and annealed in N2 at 900 °C. The resultant Nb2O5 nanocrystals offer ultrasmall size of 8.8 ± 0.6 nm and good crystallinity. The introduction of GQD realizes the construction of heterojunction. This narrows bandgap energy of Nb2O5 and optimizes the band structure. The narrowed bandgap energy leads to an enhanced visible light absorption, and more negative conduction band potential and more positive valence band potential promote production of superoxide (•O2 –) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH). Combination of graphene with GQD improves adsorption of ciprofloxacin, separation of generated electrons and holes and light absorption capacity. G-Nb2O5-900 exhibits an excellent photocatalytic performance for solar light driven photodegradation of ciprofloxacin. The photodegradation efficiency is more than 3.58 times under ultraviolet light radiation and 12.4 times under visible light radiation over pristine Nb2O5. G-Nb2O5-900 shows a broad application prospect in antibiotic treatment in environmental water.

Plots (A) of adsorption efficiency of ciprofloxacin in dark and photodegradation efficiency of ciprofloxacin under solar light radiation and visible light radiation in the absence of photocatalyst and the presence of Nb2O5 and G-Nb2O5-900 vs. the time and corresponding photodegradation rates of ciprofloxacin (B), plots (C) of ciprofloxacin removal efficiency vs. the time with G-Nb2O5-900 by using the adsorption-photodegradation mode and the direct photodegradation mode under solar light radiation, and photodegradation rates of ciprofloxacin with G-Nb2O5-900 for five reuses.

Plots (A) of adsorption-photodegradation efficiency of different concentration of ciprofloxacin with G-Nb2O5-900 vs. the time and the corresponding first-order kinetic curves (B), the adsorption rate in dark and photodegradation rate of ciprofloxacin under solar light radiation with G-Nb2O5-900 at different pH conditions (C), and the photodegradation rates of 10 mg mL−1 ciprofloxacin in ultrapure water, seawater, river water and aquaculture effluent with G-Nb2O5-900 under 100 min of solar light radiation (D).

head.ce:title: Nb2O5-graphene heterojunction composite with ultrahigh photocatalytic activity for solar light driven photodegradation of ciprofloxacin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, School of Life Science and Health Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The paper reported synthesis of Nb2O5-graphene heterojunction photocatalyst with functional graphene quantum dot. The photocatalyst exhibits ultrahigh photocatalytic activity for solar light driven photodegradation of ciprofloxacin.

Poor visible light absorption capacity and intrinsic conductivity restrict many applications of Nb2O5 in photocatalysis. The paper describes one solution for synthesizing Nb2O5-graphene photocatalyst with functional graphene quantum dot (GQD). Firstly, Nb(V) was coordinated with GQD to form water-soluble complex. Then, it was bound to graphene sheet via π-π stacking, reduced with ascorbic acid to produce graphene oxide gel and annealed in N2 at 900 °C. The resultant Nb2O5 nanocrystals offer ultrasmall size of 8.8 ± 0.6 nm and good crystallinity. The introduction of GQD realizes the construction of heterojunction. This narrows bandgap energy of Nb2O5 and optimizes the band structure. The narrowed bandgap energy leads to an enhanced visible light absorption, and more negative conduction band potential and more positive valence band potential promote production of superoxide (•O2 –) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH). Combination of graphene with GQD improves adsorption of ciprofloxacin, separation of generated electrons and holes and light absorption capacity. G-Nb2O5-900 exhibits an excellent photocatalytic performance for solar light driven photodegradation of ciprofloxacin. The photodegradation efficiency is more than 3.58 times under ultraviolet light radiation and 12.4 times under visible light radiation over pristine Nb2O5. G-Nb2O5-900 shows a broad application prospect in antibiotic treatment in environmental water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Ciprofloxacin is a widely used fluoroquinolone antibiotics to control bacterial infection. However, the most of ciprofloxacin ingested by human and animal was directly excreted into the environment due to high chemical stability [1]. An excessive ciprofloxacin causes drug resistance in human and animal and damaging immune function [2,3]. Development of an efficient technique for removing ciprofloxacin from environment water is important to ensure human health. But traditional adsorption, biodegradation and Fenton treatment are difficult to be used for the removal of ciprofloxacin from environment water due to the lack of sufficient efficiency [4]. Photodegradation has received a great interest in ciprofloxacin treatment because of its high efficiency, low-cost and environmental friendliness [5].

Many photocatalysts have been developed for solar light driven photodegradation of organic pollutants such as g-C3N4 and TiO2-based semiconductors [6–9]. Pristine g-C3N4 possesses physicochemical stability, cost-effectiveness, easy synthesis, material abundance, environmental friendliness and good band structure. The band structure can offer a sufficiently high valence band potential for generating highly active hydroxyl radical and a sufficiently low conduction band potential for generating highly active superoxide radical. However, g-C3N4 shows a poor catalytic activity because of its fast photogenerated charge recombination, low specific surface area and weak oxidation–reduction ability. Especially wide bandgap energy makes absorption ability of g-C3N4 to visible light is very weak [10]. To resolve these problems, some semiconductors were used for construction of heterojunctions, including CeO2 [11], Bi2WO6 and CuS [12], Fe3O4 [13], NdFe2O4 [14] and ZnO [15]. The construction of heterojunction narrows the bandgap energy of g-C3N4 and creates new charge transfer pathway. In addition, the electric field in heterojunction also promotes the separation of photogenerated charge carriers. Alshaikh et al. reported synthesis of S-scheme mesoporous Ag3VO4/g-C3N4 heterojunction photocatalyst [16]. The S-scheme heterojunction structure improves the photocatalytic performance. In addition to constructing heterojunction, defect engineering is another way to improve photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4. The presence of vacancies narrows the bandgap energy and leads to an enhanced photocatalytic activity. Kundu et al. reported synthesis of La3+-decorated two-dimensional g-C3N4 photocatalyst [17]. The defect engineering produced by La3+ doping reduces bandgap energy of g-C3N4 to 2.21 eV. This makes photodegradation efficiency of ciprofloxacin is up to 93% under the ultraviolet light irradiation. Compared to g-C3N4, TiO2 has a better structural stability. TiO2 has become the most widely studied photocatalyst because of non-toxicity, low-cost and chemical stability [18]. However, TiO2 can only utilize the ultraviolet light in solar light because of its wide bandgap energy. As the energy of ultraviolet light is about 4% of total solar light energy, pristine TiO2 always exhibits a low photocatalytic activity under solar light radiation. Lu et al. reported one dual Z-scheme Bi2MoO6/GQDs/TiO2 heterojunction photocatalyst for photodegradation of ciprofloxacin [19]. The photocatalyst exhibits 90.21% of photodegradation efficiency and 0.01549 min−1 of rate constant. These are higher than that of Bi2MoO6/TiO2, Bi2MoO6 and TiO2. The result demonstrates that the construction of Z-scheme heterojunction can improve the photocatalytic performance of TiO2. Nb2O5 has attracted a more interestin photodegradation because of its low toxicity, good structural stability and relatively high photocatalytic activity. Different from TiO2, Nb2O5 photocatalyst provides a better band structure that promote production of superoxide (•O2 –) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH). However, poor visible light absorption capacity and intrinsic conductivity still prevent the application of Nb2O5 in ciprofloxacin photodegradation.

Several strategies have been established for improving the photocatalytic performance of Nb2O5. The first strategy is to fabricate the Nb2O5 nanostructure with an interesting morphology such as nanowire [20], nanobelt [21], nanorod [22], nanotube [23] and hollow nanospheres [24]. The special morphology offers a better photocatalytic activity for photocatalytic reaction. Zhao et al. synthesized Nb2O5 nanorods by hydrothermal treatment [25]. As the formed Nb2O5 nanorod offers more exposed (001) crystal plane, its photocatalytic activity for the oxidation of methylene blue is more than 2-fold that of Nb2O5 nanosphere. The second strategy is to introduce special metal ion into Nb2O5 nanocrystal. Ucker et al. reported the construction of Fe inserted in Nb2O5 [26]. The Fe ion insertion brings an obvious change in microscopic, surface and optical properties. The bandgap energy decreases with the increase of Fe concentration. This leads to an enhanced visible light absorption capacity. The third strategy is to construct various heterojunction semiconductors that offer more efficient charge transfer pathway. Li et al. reported one Nb2O5/g-C3N4 heterojunction photocatalyst for catalytic oxidation of biomass valorization [27]. The formation of heterojunction promotes the separation and transfer of photogenerated charge carriers. This realizes selective conversion of 5-hydroxymethylfurfural. Despite these advances, synthesis of highly active Nb2O5 photocatalyst still faces a great challenge.

In the study, we reported one solution for synthesizing Nb2O5-graphene photocatalyst (G-Nb2O5) by introducing graphene quantum dot (GQD) for solar light driven photodegradation of ciprofloxacin. The integration of GQD with graphene attempts to inhibit hydrolysis of Nb(V), immobilize Nb2O5 nanoparticles on graphene sheets, constructs heterojunction, accelerates separation of photogenerated charge carriers, and enhances light absorption capacity. The adsorption and photodegradation behavior of G-Nb2O5 to ciprofloxacin and photocatalytic reaction mechanism were systematically investigated.

The mixture of citric acid, glutamic acid, ethylenediamine and boric acid with the molar ratio of 1: 0.2: 0.5: 0.3 was heated in N2 atmosphere at 170 °C for 3 h and dissolved in deionized water to form GQD aqueous solution of 100 mg mL−1. It was treated by adjusting the acidity to pH 7.0 with 1.0 M NaOH solution, dialysis with a dialysis bag of 3500 Da and freeze-drying. The obtained solid GQD product was stored in dark before use [28].

NbCl5 (0.5 g) and 30 wt% H2O2 (5 mL) were added into the GQD aqueous solution (0.7 g GQD in 75 mL deionized water) with strongly stirring to form one transparent aqueous solution and then mixed with 250 mL of 4 mg mL−1 graphite oxide (GO) dispersion under ultrasound. Followed by addition of 5 g ascorbic acid and heating at 80 °C for 2 h. The collected GO gel was dried at 80 °C and then thermally annealed in N2 at different temperature for 2 h with the heating rate of 2 °C min−1. Basing on the temperature used in the thermal annealing process, the obtained products were termed as G-Nb2O5-400, G-Nb2O5-500, G-Nb2O5-600, G-Nb2O5-700, G-Nb2O5-800 and G-Nb2O5-900 in turn. These samples are black powder state solid (shown in Fig. S1). In addition, the above procedure was also used for preparation of Nb2O5 and G/GQD composite unless N2 was replaced by O2 in the thermal annealing process and no addition of NbCl5, respectively.

Photocatalytic behavior of the photocatalyst was tested by monitoring photodegradation of ciprofloxacin in water under solar light radiation or visible light radiation. The test adopted one 300 W xenon lamp as light source for simulating solar light. To simulate visible light, the lamp was equipped with ultraviolet cut-off filter (k greater than 420 nm). Typically, 50 mg of photocatalyst was dispersed in 100 mL of deionized water containing 15 mg L-1 ciprofloxacin. Before light radiation, the suspension was stirred in a dark for 2 h to reach an adsorption equilibrium. After that, open the light source to trigger photodegradation process of ciprofloxacin. The photodegradation temperature was controlled at 25 °C using a circulating cooling system. In the adsorption-photodegradation process, 1.5 m L of the suspension was extracted from the system at intervals of 30 min. The collected dispersion was centrifuged to remove the photocatalyst and then used for determination of ciprofloxacin by measuring the absorbance at 273 nm. To examine the reusability of photocatalyst, five cycles of ciprofloxacin photodegradation experiments were carried out by the same procedure. After the each photodegradation test, the photocatalyst was collected by centrifugation, washing with deionized water and drying at 80 °C and then used for the next photodegradation test.

Classical three-electrode test system was used to evaluate the photochemical properties of photocatalyst. Platinum wire, Ag/AgCl stand electrode and FTO plate (1 cm × 1 cm) loading with photocatalyst were used as the counter electrode, reference electrode and working electrode, respectively. One 300 W xenon lamp was adopted as one light source and the electrolyte was a 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution.

To make a working electrode, 10 mg of photocatalyst was dispersed in the mixed solution containing 1.0 mL of ethanol and 20 μL of Nafion (10%) and then treated by ultrasound for 2 h to form a stable dispersion. Its 100 μL was dropped onto the pretreated FTO glass surface (1.0 × 1.0 cm) and subsequently dried for 2 h in a dry air to obtain one working electrode.

Transient photocurrent measurement was used to measure the steady-state photocurrent density of photocatalyst by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) in dark and 300 W xenon lamp illumination, in which the scan rate is fixed at 10 mV s−1 and the potential is at 0.4 V. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to characterize the separation and transfer of photogenerated electrons and holes, in which the potential amplitude is ± 5 mV and the frequency change range is between 0.01 Hz and 105 Hz. The Mott-Schottky curve of photocatalyst was measured at a frequency of 1000 Hz, and the flat band potential of the sample was calculated.
The pH of the photochemical solutions in the above all measurements is 7.0.
Materials and reagents, material characterization, photocatalytic active species analysis, ESR test, total organic carbon (TOC) test and cell viability were shown in ESI.

Fig. 1 A presents the scheme for synthesizing GQD and G-Nb2O5. Firstly, GQD was prepared by heating the mixture of citric acid, glutamic acid (Glu), ethylenediamine (EA) and boric acid (H3BO3) at 170 °C for 3 h. Glu and EA was used as functional reagents for introducing special chemical groups containing nitrogen and oxygen. These groups can coordinate with Nb(V) to produce water-soluble complex. H3BO3 was used as boron source for introducing boron atom into graphene sheet. As boron is an electron-deficient element, its introduction may regulate electrical and semiconductor property of GQD. The resultant GQD was characterized by TEM technique and Mott-Schottky analysis. The TEM image in Fig. S2 shows that the GQD consists of small graphene sheets with size of about 4 nm. The Mott-Schottky curve in Fig. S3 offers a positive slope, verifying that GQD is a p-type semiconductor.

The as-prepared GQD was used in synthesis of G-Nb2O5. The synthesis includes three cumulative processes. The first process is that Nb5+ is combined with the GQD by coordination bond to form stable Nb-GQD complex with the help of H2O2. The Nb-GQD complex is highly water-soluble and can disperse in water to form the transparent aqueous solution. Hence, the coordination can effectively prevent hydrolysis of Nb(V) during the synthesis of G-Nb2O5. Compared with the reported ethylene diamine tetra-acetic acid and boron-functionalized graphene quantum dot (p-GQD) [29], the GQD has a much better water-solubility and ability to the coordination with Nb5+. The characteristics make the GQD can preferably prevent the hydrolysis of high concentration of Nb5+ ions in the aqueous medium. This not only enhance the reaction efficiency for synthesis of G-Nb2O5 composite but also reduce the energy consumption. The second process is to bind Nb-GQD complex to graphene sheet for forming Nb-GQD/GO composite via the π-π stacking. Then, the Nb-GQD/GO composite was reduced to form GO gel by ascorbic acid. Because of significant decrease in water solubility, the GO gel rapidly precipitates from the reaction system. The formation of GO gel helps to realize the spatial separation of Nb(V) ions and finally leads to formation of ultrasmall Nb2O5 nanocrystals. This improves the dispersion of Nb2O5 nanocrystals on graphene sheets. The third process is to anneal the GO gel in N2 to produce G-Nb2O5 composite. At a relatively high temperature, Nb(V) ions will be changed into Nb2O5 nanocrystals. Meanwhile, GO and GQD in the reaction system were reduced into graphene. This leads to an improved conductivity of G-Nb2O5-900. To calculate the content of graphene in the final product, the above synthesis procedure was also used to treat the GO with the same mass used in the synthesis of G-Nb2O5-900 unless no addition of GQD and NbCl5. The result indicates that the actual loading amounts of graphene in the G-Nb2O5-900 sample is 67.2 wt%.

The as-synthesized G-Nb2O5-900 was characterized by SEM, TEM, high angle annular dark field STEM (HAADF-STEM), EDS element mapping, XRD, Raman spectrum, IR spectrum, UV–visible absorption spectrum and XPS spectrum. Fig. 1B-J present the SEM, enlarged TEM, HAADF-STEM and EDS elemental mappings of niobium (Nb), oxygen (O), nitrogen (N), carbon (C) and merged element of G-Nb2O5-900. The SEM and TEM analysis reveal that the G-Nb2O5-900 offers one three-dimensional structure with the rich of large and small pores. This owes to the interweaving of graphene sheets each other. The enlarged TEM image contains a large number of black dots. These black dots may be Nb2O5 nanocrystals and small graphene sheets. The HAADF-STEM image appears many bright dots. Because niobium atom gives the largest atomic weight in G-Nb2O5-900, these bright spots present the Nb2O5 nanocrystals. The HAADF-STEM analysis also demonstrates that the Nb2O5 nanocrystal shows one spherical nanostructure with an average particle size of 8.8 ± 0.6 nm. Ultrasmall size increases number of active sites on the surface of Nb2O5 nanocrystal and leads to an improved photocatalytic activity. All Nb2O5 nanocrystals were well dispersed on the surface of three-dimensional graphene framework. Such a good dispersion could be attributed to the introduction of GQD. Due to strong π-π stacking force between graphene oxide and GQD, all Nb(V) ions were firmly immobilized on graphene sheets. This can effectively prevent agglomeration of Nb(V) ions and results in formation of Nb2O5 nanocrystals with a good dispersion. Further, the result of EDS element mappings confirms the spatial distribution of Nb, O, N and C elements on the surface of G-Nb2O5-900. Since the boron content in final product is extremely low, the boron element cannot be almost seen in the element mapping. The Nb element only originates from Nb2O5. The N element only originates from GQD. The C element come from both graphene and GQD. Thus, the results of element mapping analysis also confirms that the G-Nb2O5-900 contains Nb2O5, GQD and graphene.

Fig. 2 presents the XRD pattern, Raman spectra and FTIR spectrum of G-Nb2O5-900 and UV–visible absorption spectrum of G/GQD composite. The XRD pattern includes ten diffraction peaks at 23.18°, 26.00°, 30.45°, 33.18°, 39.88°, 43.44°, 47.01°, 52.16°, 57.67° and 67.98° (Fig. 2A). The peaks at 23.18°, 30.45°, 33.18°, 47.01°, 52.16° and 57.67° correspond to the (001), (100), (181), (002) and (182) crystal facets of orthorhombic Nb2O5. All peaks can be matched with the diffraction peaks in JCPDS No. 30–0873. This verifies that our synthesis achieves to the formation of T-phase Nb2O5 nanocrystals [30]. The wide peak at 26.00° corresponds to the (002) crystal facet of graphene. Due to poor crystallinity of graphene, the peak at 26.0° displays a weaker and broader characteristic [30]. The Raman spectrum of G-Nb2O5-900 includes three weak Raman peaks at 72, 243 and 604 cm−1 and two strong Raman peaks at 1312 and 1608 cm−1 (Fig. 2B). The weak Raman peaks at 72, 243 and 604 cm−1 owe to the Raman absorption of Nb2O5 in G-Nb2O5-900. The peaks at 243 and 604 cm−1 belong to the Nb-O stretching of long and intermediate bond along with symmetry-related vibrations. The vibration mode at frequency of 243 cm−1 is assigned to the T2u and while peak at 604 cm−1 is due to the overlap of T1u bands [31]. The strong peaks at 1312 and 1608 cm−1 can be assigned to the D band and G band of graphene. The D-band is caused by defects and breakdown of translation symmetry and breathing mode of aromatic rings A1g symmetry. The G-band is due to the first-order scattering sp2 domain of graphite. Basing on the data in Fig. 2B, the ID/IG value was calculated. The result shows that the ID/IG value is about 1.86 for G-Nb2O5-900. Such a higher ID/IG value presents the exposure of more defect and sides of graphene sheets. The IR absorption spectrum includes several absorption bands and peaks between 500 and 4000 cm−1 (Fig. 2C). The strong and wide absorption band between 4000 and 3000 cm−1 is the IR absorption produced by symmetric vibration of N-H, O-H and C-H bonds. The weak peak at 2922 cm−1 is produced by the IR absorption of symmetric vibration of –C–H bond. The moderately strong peak at 1662 cm−1 owes to the stretching vibration of CO and CC bonds. The strong peak at 1092 cm−1 is caused by the IR absorption of out-of-plane bending vibration of O-H bond. The moderately strong peak at 713 cm−1 is the out-of-plane bending vibration of Nb-O-Nb bond. The above results demonstrate that G-Nb2O5-900 contains –OH, –NH2, –NH- and –COOH groups. As –NH2 and –NH– groups only come from GQD, the above results also confirm that G-Nb2O5-900 contains GQD.

To understand the role of graphene and GQD to improve the light absorption capacity of G-Nb2O5-900, UV–visible absorption spectrum of the G/GQD composite was measured. Fig. 2D shows that the UV–visible absorption spectrum of G/GQD composite shows several absorption peaks at 280, 436 and 523 nm (Fig. 2D). The strong peak at 280 nm is mainly due to light absorption of chemical groups in GQD. The absorption between 350 and 750 nm is due to light absorption of graphene sheets from GQD and graphene. The result of UV–visible absorption analysis demonstrates that the G/GQD composite has a strong absorption ability towards UV light and visible light. This enhances light absorption capacity of G-Nb2O5-900 during photodegradation.

Fig. 3 presents the total XPS spectrum of G-Nb2O5-900 and high resolution XPS spectra of B1s, Nb3d, C1s, N1s and O1s. The total XPS spectrum includes Nb4d, N4s, Nb3d, B1s, C1s, N1s, Nb3s and O1s peaks (Fig. 3A). This demonstrates that G-Nb2O5-900 is mainly composed of Nb, B, C, N and O elements. Further, the atomic weight percentages for elemental composition in G-Nb2O5-900 composite was obtained by basing on XPS characterization-EDS. Table S2 shows the atomic weight percentages of Nb, C, N, O and B are 14.73, 76.93, 8.01, 0.33 and 0.01, respectively. High resolution spectrum of B1s could be divided into three peaks at 191.16, 191.48 and 192.14 eV, which correspond to B-C, BC2O and BCO2 (Fig. 3B). The B-C was formed by doping boron into carbon lattice of graphene sheets from GQD. BC2O and BCO2 are owed to dope boron into the edge of graphene sheets in GQD. High resolution XPS spectrum of Nd3d could be divided into two peaks at 206.71 and 209.41 eV (Fig. 3C), which correspond to the Nd3d1/2 and Nd3d3/2, respectively [32]. High resolution XPS spectrum of C1s could be divided into four peaks at 284.55, 285.30, 286.20 and 289.05 eV (Fig. 3D), which correspond to the C-C, C-O, C-O-C and C = O bonds. High resolution XPS spectrum of N1s could be divided into two peaks at 399.26 and 399.78 eV (Fig. 3E), which correspond to the pyrrolic N and amine N respectively. The result demonstrates the nitrogen-containing groups are mainly distributed at the edges of graphene sheets. The high resolution XPS spectrum of O1s could be divided into three peaks at 532.01, 533.43 and 536.86 eV (Fig. 3F), which correspond to the Nb-O, OH/CO3 and H2O/C = O.

To evaluate semiconductor property of G-Nb2O5-900, UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) and Mott-Schottky plots of Nb2O5 and G-Nb2O5-900 were investigated. Fig. 4 A-C present DRS, plots of transformed Kubelka-Munk function vs. the energy of light, and Mott-Schottky plots for Nb2O5 and G-Nb2O5-900. It can be seen from Fig. 4A that the UV–visible absorption spectrum of Nb2O5 is mainly located in the ultraviolet region, verifying Nb2O5 only has a weak absorption capacity to visible light. Thus, pristine Nb2O5 can use ultraviolet light and cannot effectively utilize the visible light in solar light. The reduced GQD shows one strong light absorption ability in a wide spectrum range of 200–800 nm. Compared to Nb2O5, G-Nb2O5-900 offers a stronger and wider light absorption spectrum. This demonstrates that the introduction of GQD greatly enhances the absorption capacity of Nb2O5 towards visible light. Basing on the Kubelka-Munk function vs. the energy of light, the bandgap energy (Eg) of semiconductor material can be estimated (Fig. 4B). The result shows that the Eg values are 3.18 eV of Nb2O5 and 2.74 eV of G-Nb2O5-900. These data verify that the introduction of graphene and GQD narrows the bandgap energy of Nb2O5. This enhances visible light absorption capacity of Nb2O5. The conduction band potential (ECB) can be obtained by tangents of the Mott-Schottky plots of Nb2O5 and G-Nb2O5-900 extension with the abscissa axis (Fig. 4C). The results indicates that the ECB values are −1.18 V vs. the Ag/AgCl for Nb2O5 (−0.75 V vs. SHE) and −1.15 V vs. Ag/AgCl for G-Nb2O5-900 (−0.72 V vs. the SHE) [33]. The valence band potentials (EVB) were calculated by using the following formula: EVB (V vs. SHE) = Eg + ECB. The results indicate the EVB values are 2.43 V of Nb2O5 and 2.02 V of G-Nb2O5-900. The reduced band gap energy for G-Nb2O5-900 can bring a faster separation of photogenerated electrons and holes. To confirm the result, the fluorescence spectra and Nyquist plots of Nb2O5 and G-Nb2O5-900 were measured. Fig. S1 shows the photoluminescence spectra of Nb2O5 and G-Nb2O5-900 under excitation of 335 nm. The fluorescence intensity can reflect recombination rate of photogenerated electrons and holes. A lower fluorescence intensity presents a slower recombination process of photogenerated carriers [34]. The Nb2O5 shows the stronger fluorescence intensity, verifying that the Nb2O5 offers the faster recombination process of photogenerated electrons and holes. However, the introduction of GQD and graphene results in a reduced fluorescence intensity. Due to good conductivity, the reduced GQD and graphene can rapidly capture the photogenerated electrons. This effectively suppresses the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. Fig. 4D presents the Nyquist plots of Nb2O5 and G-Nb2O5-900 in 0.2 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution. Basing on the equivalent circuit in the Insert in Fig. 4D, the EIS parameters were calculated and the results were listed in Table S1. Table S1 indicates the charge transfer resistance (Rct) is 15.96 Ω, indicating a fast charge transfer process. The value is much lower than that of Nb2O5 (6187 Ω), verifying that the introduction of graphene and GQD greatly accelerates the interfacial charge transfer rate. Therefore, the G/GQD composite can be used as an electron trapper to promote the photogenerated charge transfer during photocatalytic process [35].

Fig. 5 presents the energy band structure of G-Nb2O5-900 and transfer mechanism of photogenerated charge carriers for solar light driven photodegradation of ciprofloxacin. Since Nb2O5 is n-type semiconductor, the GQD is p-type semiconductor and graphene is one conductor, their intimate contacts lead to formation of PN-heterojunction at the Nb2O5/GQD interface and Schottky heterojunction at the GQD/graphene interface. The formation of heterojunctions narrows the bandgap energy of Nb2O5 to 2.74 eV from 3.18 eV and results in an enhanced visible light absorption capacity. Compared to TiO2, G-Nb2O5-900 shows a more proper structure of energy band [36]. The ECB potential is −0.72 eV, which is more negative than the potential of O2/•O2 –. Thus, G-Nb2O5-900 can catalyze reduction of O2 dissolved in water and produces a large number of superoxide radicals (•O2 –). The EVB potential is 2.02 eV, which is more positive than the potential of •OH/OH– [37]. Thus, G-Nb2O5-900 can catalyze oxidation of OH– and produces hydroxyl radicals (•OH). The synergy of highly active
·
O 2 -
, •OH and photogenerated hole (h+) realizes an efficient photodegradation of ciprofloxacin. In addition, the G/GQD composite plays important roles in photodegradation of ciprofloxacin. The first role is to use as an ideal adsorbent to improve the adsorption ability of Nb2O5 towards ciprofloxacin. Due to large specific surface and π-electron conjugation system, the G/GQD composite offers strong adsorption capacity towards ciprofloxacin. Thus, the presence of G/GQD composite can ensure ciprofloxacin and photogenerated active species undergo in situ oxidation and reduction on the surface of G-Nb2O5-900. This brings an improved photocatalytic activity. The second role is to use as an electron trapper to promote separation of photogenerated electrons and holes. Due to high conductivity, the presence of G/GQD composite accelerates separation of photogenerated electrons and holes. This will effectively prevent recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. The third role is to use a light absorption antenna to enhance light absorption capacity of G-Nb2O5-900. Thus, G/GQD can strongly absorb solar light in a wide spectrum range from ultraviolet light to visible light. This improves the light absorption capacity of G-Nb2O5-900 and leads to an enhanced photocatalytic activity under solar light radiation. Therefore, G-Nb2O5-900 exhibits a better photocatalytic performance for solar light driven photodegradation of ciprofloxacin compared with pristine Nb2O5.

To examine the photocatalytic activity of G-Nb2O5-900, photoelectrochemical H2 generation in dark and 300 W xenon lamp illumination were measured by linear sweep voltammogram. Fig. 6 shows linear sweep voltammogram plots of Nb2O5 and G-Nb2O5-900 in dark and 300 W xenon lamp illumination. Both Nb2O5 and G-Nb2O5-900 only can produce a low dark current, verifying that the photoelectrochemical H2 generation cannot undergo in dark. However, the light illumination causes an obvious increase in the photocurrent density. Interestingly, the photocurrent density of G-Nb2O5-900 is up to 3.56 mA cm−2 under the illumination, which is more than 6.4-fold that of pristine Nb2O5. This confirms that G-Nb2O5-900 has a better photocatalytic activity compared to pristine Nb2O5.

Ciprofloxacin was employed as one model compound to evaluate the adsorption and photodegradation behavior of G-Nb2O5-900. Fig. 7 A and B presents the plots of adsorption efficiency of ciprofloxacin in dark and photodegradation efficiency of ciprofloxacin under solar light radiation vs. the time with different photocatalysts and corresponding maximum adsorption/degradation rates. The concentration of ciprofloxacin for all plots decreases in dark with the increase of time when the time is less than 15 min. This is owed to the adsorption of graphene and GQD towards ciprofloxacin. Ciprofloxacin contains one two-dimensional aromatic ring with a large π-electron conjugation system. Thus, it can be firmly bound to graphene sheets via the π-π stacking. This causes a reduce in the concentration of ciprofloxacin in an aqueous solution. Good adsorption behavior is conductive to achieving in situ photodegradation of ciprofloxacin on the surface of photocatalyst. When the time is more than 15 min, the change slows down, reaches to the maximum and then tends to stabilize, indicating that the adsorption process reaches the thermodynamic equilibrium. However, Fig. 7A and B show different photocatalyst offers a different adsorption ability towards ciprofloxacin. The Nb2O5 offers a poor adsorption ability towards ciprofloxacin. Its adsorption rate is about 1.2%. The introduction brings an obvious increase of adsorption rate. Among various G-Nb2O5 products, the maximum adsorption rate of G-Nb2O5-900 is obviously higher than that of G-Nb2O5-400, G-Nb2O5-500, G-Nb2O5-600, G-Nb2O5-700 and G-Nb2O5-800. At a higher temperature for the annealing, GQD and graphene in the G-Nb2O5 can be more fully reduced. The reduction not only enhances the electron density of graphene sheets due to the stripping of electron withdrawing groups in a high temperature such as carboxyl groups but also improves the planarity of GQDs. Consequently, the G-Nb2O5-900 can firmly bind with ciprofloxacin because of the intimate contact between graphene and ciprofloxacin and high charge density on graphene sheet. The strong interaction results in an improved adsorption behavior. Similar to the adsorption process, the photodegradation rate of G-Nb2O5-900 under solar light radiation is better than that of G-Nb2O5-400, G-Nb2O5-500, G-Nb2O5-600, G-Nb2O5-700 and G-Nb2O5-800. The removal rate of ciprofloxacin is close to 100%, verifying that G-Nb2O5-900 has the best photocatalytic activity.

Fig. 7C and D show the first-order kinetic curves of solar light driven ciprofloxacin photodegradation with different photocatalysts and corresponding rate constant (k0). All photodegradation reactions with different photocatalysts conform to the first order reaction kinetics. However, the K0 value with different photocatalyst exists an obvious difference. The k0 of G-Nb2O5-900 reaches to 0.0142 min−1, which is more than 1.69-fold that of G-Nb2O5-800, 2.78-fold that of G-Nb2O5-700, 4.3-fold that of G-Nb2O5-600, 8.8-fold that of G-Nb2O5-500 and 23.6-fold that of G-Nb2O5-400. This demonstrates that G-Nb2O5-900 offers the best photocatalytic activity. The enhanced photocatalytic activity is due to strong adsorption ability and good conductivity of G-Nb2O5-900. On the one hand, G-Nb2O5-900 has an excellent adsorption ability towards ciprofloxacin. This ensures ciprofloxacin can be oxidized and reduced in situ on the surface of G-Nb2O5-900. On the other hand, G-Nb2O5-900 has a higher conductivity because of a fuller reduction of graphene and GQD in G-Nb2O5-900. As an electron trapper, a higher conductivity of G/GQD composite brings a faster separation process of photogenerated electrons and holes. The above two factors improve the photocatalytic activity of G-Nb2O5-900 under solar light radiation.

Fig. 8 A and B show plots of adsorption efficiency of ciprofloxacin in dark and photodegradation efficiency of ciprofloxacin under solar light radiation and visible light radiation in the absence of photocatalyst and the presence of Nb2O5 and G-Nb2O5-900 vs. the time and corresponding photodegradation rates of ciprofloxacin. It can be seen from Fig. 8A that the adsorption rate of Nb2O5 to ciprofloxacin is close to zero, indicating a poor adsorption capacity. This is due to the lack of strong interaction between Nb2O5 and ciprofloxacin. The result also demonstrates that the high adsorption efficiency of G-Nb2O5-900 to ciprofloxacin is attributed to the introduction of graphene and GQD. In the absence of photocatalyst, the photodegradation rate of ciprofloxacin is close zero, verifying that ciprofloxacin cannot be degraded under solar light radiation. Thus, the self-degradation of ciprofloxacin can be negligible. In the presence of Nb2O5, the photodegradation rate of ciprofloxacin is 7.28% under visible light radiation. This is due to a wide band gap energy of Nb2O5 (3.18 eV). Such a wide band gap energy makes Nb2O5 can only offer a low absorption capacity to visible light. Thus, the pristine Nb2O5 exhibits a low photocatalytic activity under visible light radiation. Compared to visible light, the solar light radiation produces a higher photodegradation rate (26.4%). This is owed to strong ultraviolet light absorption capacity of Nb2O5. In the presence of G-Nb2O5-900, the photodegradation rate reaches to 94.6% under solar light radiation, which is more than 3.58 times that of the pristine Nb2O5. This demonstrates that the introduction of G/GQD composite enhances the photocatalytic activity of Nb2O5. Interestingly, the photodegradation rate still remains 90.48% under visible light radiation, which is more than 12.4-fold that of the pristine Nb2O5. The result indicates the introduction of graphene and GQD greatly improves light absorption capacity of Nb2O5 to visible light. To save the time for the ciprofloxacin treatment, the photodegradation of ciprofloxacin with G-Nb2O5-900 under solar light radiation was investigated by using the adsorption-photodegradation mode and direct photodegradation mode. Fig. 8C indicates the direct photodegradation mode requires a much less than time for the ciprofloxacin treatment compared with the adsorption-photodegradation mode. It only takes 100 min to achieve ciprofloxacin photodegradation of almost 100%. However, to achieve the same effect, the adsorption-photodegradation mode takes 300 min at least. A reduced treatment time could be attributed to a fast photodegradation process. As the photodegradation rate of ciprofloxacin is a much bigger that of ciprofloxacin adsorption on the surface of G-Nb2O5-900, ciprofloxacin molecules cannot accumulate in large quantities on the surface of photocatalyst. This will reduce the energy loss of solar light radiation caused by absorption of ciprofloxacin to solar light and leads to an improved photodegradation efficiency. In addition, the reuse of G-Nb2O5-900 for solar light driven photodegradation of ciprofloxacin was also studied. Fig. 8D indicates five repeated uses of G-Nb2O5-900 did not bring a significant changes in photodegradation efficiency of ciprofloxacin, indicating a good reusability. Good reusability is due to high structural stability of G-Nb2O5-900. On the one hand, the result of XRD analysis confirms that the as-prepared Nb2O5 nanoparticles has a good crystallinity. This improves the structural stability of Nb2O5 nanoparticles and leads to a stable photodegradation efficiency. On the other hand, the three-dimensional graphene framework gives a good mechanical property of G-Nb2O5-900. The characteristic makes G-Nb2O5-900 can rapidly release the stresses produced by the photodegradation reaction and maintains the structural stability of G-Nb2O5-900. The synergy of above two factors achieves to a good reusability of G-Nb2O5-900.

To test the practicality of G-Nb2O5-900 in photodegradation of ciprofloxacin in environmental water, the effect of ciprofloxacin concentration, medium pH and kind of water sample on adsorption-photodegradation efficiency were investigated. Fig. 9 A indicates plots of adsorption-photodegradation efficiency for different concentration of ciprofloxacin with G-Nb2O5-900 vs. the time. When the ciprofloxacin concentration is 5 mg mL−1, all ciprofloxacin molecules are rapidly adsorbed on the surface of G-Nb2O5-900 in dark. When the ciprofloxacin concentration is up to 10, 15 and 20 mg mL−1, G-Nb2O5-900 shows a high adsorption efficiency of ciprofloxacin and a high photodegradation efficiency of ciprofloxacin. The total removal rates of ciprofloxacin reach to 96.8% of 10 mg mL−1, 94.6% of 15 mg mL−1 and 88.6% of 20 mg mL−1. However, continue to increase the concentration of ciprofloxacin causes a significant reduce in adsorption efficiency and photodegradation efficiency. This is because G-Nb2O5-900 cannot provide enough active sites for adsorption and photodegradation of ciprofloxacin. Thus, appropriately increase the amounts of G-Nb2O5-900 is necessary to obtain an ideal removal efficiency when the water sample contains a high concentration of ciprofloxacin. Further, Fig. 9B indicates the first-order kinetic curves of solar light driven photodegradation of different concentration of ciprofloxacin with G-Nb2O5-900. The slope of first-order curve presents the corresponding rate constant (k0). The k0 values are 0.0147 min−1 of 10 mg mL−1 ciprofloxacin, 0.0129 min−1 of 15 mg mL−1 ciprofloxacin, 0.0055 min−1 of 20 mg mL−1 ciprofloxacin, 0.0022 min−1 of 25 mg mL−1 ciprofloxacin and 0.0002 min−1 of 20 mg mL−1 ciprofloxacin. These data indicate that photodegradation rate reduces with the increase of ciprofloxacin concentration.

Fig. 9C presents adsorption rate in dark and photodegradation rate of ciprofloxacin under solar light radiation with G-Nb2O5-900 at different pH values. Both the adsorption rate and the photodegradation rate at pH 3 are obviously lower than that at pH 5, 7 and 9. This is due to the dissociation and protonation of ciprofloxacin in water. At an acid medium of pH 3, ciprofloxacin molecules partly occur the protonation to form positively charged cations. This will weaken the interaction between ciprofloxacin and G-Nb2O5-900 caused by the π-π stacking due to the decrease in the electron density of ciprofloxacin, leading to a reduced adsorption rate. At pH values of 5, 7 and 9, the removal rate of ciprofloxacin are 74.7%, 92.6% and 86.7%, respectively. The data reveal that G-Nb2O5-900 at a wide pH range of 5–9 can offer a high removal efficiency of ciprofloxacin.

Fig. 9D shows photodegradation rates of 10 mg mL−1 ciprofloxacin in ultrapure water, seawater, river water and aquaculture effluent with G-Nb2O5-900 by 100 min of solar light radiation. It can be seen from Fig. 9D that all photodegradation rates are more than 60%. The proves that G-Nb2O5-900 has a relatively high photocatalytic activity for solar light driven photodegradation of ciprofloxacin in various kinds of environmental waters. Fig. 9D also indicates that the photodegradation in different kinds of water samples exist an obvious difference. In the ultrapure water the photodegradation rate of ciprofloxacin is up to 94.6%, which is better than that in aquaculture effluent (90.2%), river water (75.4%) and seawater (60.5%). The difference is mainly due to the metal cations in water. The investigation has demonstrated that some metal cations can combine with
·
O 2 -
to form complex. This will consume the
·
O 2 -
free radicals produced by the G-Nb2O5-900 under solar light excitation, resulting in a decrease in the photodegradation efficiency.

To understand photodegradation mechanism of ciprofloxacin, several quenchers, including citric acid, H2O2, isopropanol and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidinooxy (Tempol), were used to capture h+, e-, •OH and •O2 – produced by G-Nb2O5-900 under solar light radiation. Fig. 10 A presents in the absence of quencher the photodegradation rate reaches 94.81%, showing a high photocatalytic activity. However, the presence of citric acid, H2O2, isopropanol and Tempol produces a significant decrease in photodegradation rate. This demonstrates that h+, e-, •OH and •O2 – are main active species in the photocatalytic reaction. Further, the active species during photocatalytic process were determined by ESR technique. One 1:2:2:1 characteristic peak of •OH appears in Fig. 10B, verifying the existence of •OH free radical. One 1:1:1:1 characteristic peak of •O2 – appears in Fig. 10D, verifying the existence of •O2 – free radicals. However, no characteristic peak of 1O2 appears in Fig. 10C. The result demonstrates again that 1O2 is not main active specie and only plays a minor role in the photodegradation of ciprofloxacin.

The above results demonstrate that G-Nb2O5-900 can produce h+, e-, •OH and •O2 – under solar light radiation. Due to their strong oxidizing and reducing ability, ciprofloxacin will be decomposed into various fragments and finally degraded into CO2 and H2O by photocatalysis of G-Nb2O5-900 [15]. To confirm the suggest, total organic carbon (TOC) test was performed by degrading ciprofloxacin solution (10 mg L-1, 100 mL) with 20 mg of G-Nb2O5-900. After 100 min of solar light radiation, 15 mL of the reaction solution was taken out and detected by TOC analyzer. The results show that the TOC removal rate is lower than that of ciprofloxacin degradation. The G-Nb2O5-900 performs the highest removal rate of TOC at 100 min is about 82.5%. However, the photodegradation efficiency of G-Nb2O5-900 is close to 100%, which is 17.5% lower than that of TOC removal rate. The discrepancy is due to the existence of some intermediate of ciprofloxacin substances, which weren’t completely broken or mineralized into CO2 and H2O. The TOC removal rate with pristine Nb2O5 is 15.6%, which is lower than that with G-Nb2O5-900. This demonstrates that G-Nb2O5-900 can produce more h+, e-, •OH and •O2 – under solar light radiation compared with pristine Nb2O5. Further, the cell viability test was used to assess the environmental deterioration due to toxins. The result indicates that the non-photodegrade ciprofloxacin (control) was only 42.8% of cell viability under solar light radiation, indicating a high toxicity towards MGC-803 cells. However, the cell viability will gradually increase with the increase of solar light irradiation time. The cell viability is 85.1, 90.3, 95.2, and 100.4% for 100, 120, 140 and 160 min of the photodegradation reaction, respectively. Basically, the cell viability greater than 80% is considered nontoxic to the cell. The cell viability test shows that G-Nb2O5-900 photodegraded ciprofloxacin after 160 min solar light irradiation shows no toxicity to MGC-803 cells.

The study demonstrates one solution for synthesizing Nb2O5-graphene heterojunction composite. The introduction of glutamic acid and ethylenediamine-functionalized and boron-doped graphene quantum dot achieves to the inhibition of Nb(V) hydrolysis and construction of PN heterojunction and Schottky heterojunction. The formation of heterojunction narrows the bandgap energy and leads to an enhanced light absorption capacity towards visible light. The integration of graphene with graphene quantum dot realizes an intimate contact of Nb2O5 with ciprofloxacin, fast separation of photogenerated charge carriers and large absorption capacity towards solar light. The Nb2O5-graphene photocatalyst exhibits excellent photocatalytic performances for the photodegradation of ciprofloxacin.
Li Ruiyi: Investigation. Liu Xiaoyue: Investigation. Li Zaijun: Conceptualization, investigation and Supervision.
